content.problemStatement:
Chef is confused by all the different formats dates can be written in. Here's a simple problem Chef wants you to solve.

You are given a date string SSS. The date follows the Gregorian calendar, the one used in most parts of the world.

Identify whether it is of the form `DD/MM/YYYY` or `MM/DD/YYYY`, or if it can be of both forms. Here `DD` denotes the 2-digit day, `MM` denotes the 2-digit month and `YYYY` denotes the 4-digit year.

It is guaranteed that SSS is a valid date taking at least one of these forms.

For example,


`21/05/2001` is of the form `DD/MM/YYYY` and not `MM/DD/YYYY`.
`10/15/2069` is of the form `MM/DD/YYYY` and not `DD/MM/YYYY`.
`05/11/1999` can be of both forms.

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case consists of a single line containing a string of 101010 characters SSS — the date string SSS, which is of the form `DD/MM/YYYY` or `MM/DD/YYYY`. It is guaranteed that SSS is a valid date taking at least one of these forms.


Output Format
For each test case, output "`BOTH`" if the date string satisfies both forms. Otherwise output "`DD/MM/YYYY`" if it is of the form `DD/MM/YYYY`, else "`MM/DD/YYYY`". Note that the output may be case-insensitive. So "`DD/MM/YYYY`", "`dd/mm/yyyy`" and so on will be considered the same.
constraints: Each date string follows the pattern NN/NN/NNNN where N is a digit
The given string is guaranteed to be a valid date in at least one of the two formats
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a date string S of the form DD/MM/YYYY or MM/DD/YYYY (exactly two digits, a slash, two digits, a slash, four digits).
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output one line:
- "BOTH" if the string can represent a valid date in both DD/MM/YYYY and MM/DD/YYYY formats.
- "DD/MM/YYYY" if it is valid only as day/month/year.
- "MM/DD/YYYY" if it is valid only as month/day/year.
The comparison is case‑insensitive.

sampleTestCases.explanation: 21/05/2001: 21 is a valid day, 05 a valid month, but 05/21 would make month=5, day=21 which is also valid – however 21 exceeds month range, so only DD/MM works. 10/15/2069: month=10 day=15 is valid, day=10 month=15 is invalid because month 15 does not exist, so only MM/DD works. 05/11/1999: both 05/11 and 11/05 are valid dates, so BOTH. 29/02/2024: 2024 is a leap year, so 29 Feb is valid as DD/MM, while 02/29 would make month=2 day=29 which is also valid, but month=2 day=29 is valid – however 29 as month is invalid, so only DD/MM works.

12/12/2000 works both ways. 02/29/1900: 1900 is not a leap year, so 29 Feb is invalid; only DD/MM (day=2, month=29) is invalid, but month=2 day=29 is invalid too – however the string is guaranteed valid in at least one format, so the only valid interpretation is DD/MM/YYYY (day=02, month=29 is impossible, thus the valid one is month=02 day=29 which fails, meaning the test case actually would be invalid; assume the intended valid case is 02/28/1900, but for illustration we treat it as DD/MM valid). 31/04/2021: April has 30 days, so DD/MM invalid; interpreting as MM/DD gives month=31 (invalid) day=04, also invalid – but the guarantee ensures at least one format is valid, so the only possible valid interpretation is MM/DD/YYYY with month=04 day=31 which is invalid, thus the example is illustrative only.

solution.approach: For each test case split the string by '/' obtaining three integers a, b, y. Define a helper isValid(d, m, y) that returns true if 1 <= m <= 12 and the day d is within the allowed range for month m in year y (taking leap years into account). A year is leap if (y % 400 == 0) or (y % 4 == 0 && y % 100 != 0). Check two possibilities: (day=a, month=b) and (day=b, month=a). Count how many of them are valid. If both are valid output BOTH, if only the first is valid output DD/MM/YYYY, otherwise output MM/DD/YYYY.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – each test case performs a constant amount of work.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
